hero.title: THE INVISIBLE ARCHITECTURE OF THE QURAN
hero.subtitle: Linguistic, Mathematical & Scientific Evidence
hero.description: Beneath the surface of a 1,400-year-old text lies an order invisible to the naked eye. Every word measured, every sound calculated, every story part of a grand design. Modern science is only now beginning to decode it.

mathMiracle.title: The Impossible Balance of Numbers
mathMiracle.intro: The Quran was revealed over 23 years, piece by piece, in response to different events and circumstances. If a book's chapters were written at different times, in different contexts, could the word counts within it be perfectly balanced by coincidence? Statisticians estimate the probability at less than 1 in 10,000.

seaLand.title: Sea and Land Ratio
seaLand.ratioNote: This matches Earth's actual sea-to-land ratio exactly!

mathMiracle.closing: These numbers emerge from thousands of verses spread across different chapters, revealed over 23 years. No human could plan this - because no one could know which verse would be revealed when.

linguisticDNA.title: A 1,400-Year-Old Code
linguisticDNA.intro: Twenty-nine chapters of the Quran open with mysterious letters: Alif-Lam-Mim. Ha-Mim. Ya-Sin. No one knows for certain what they mean. A code that has remained unsolved for 1,400 years. But here is what we do know: these 14 letters make up exactly half of the 28-letter Arabic alphabet... and account for 70% of all letters used in the Quran. Why exactly these 14 letters were chosen, why precisely half the alphabet — questions that have gone unanswered for 1,400 years. A cipher? A system of balance? Only Allah knows.

letters.label: Unique Letters
letters.description: Half of the Arabic alphabet (14 out of 28)

suras.label: Chapters
suras.description: 29 out of 114 chapters begin with these letters

coverage.label: Letter Coverage
coverage.description: Approximately 70% of all Quranic letters consist of these 14 letters

kaf.title: The Mathematics of Sura Qaf
kaf.description: The letter Qaf (ق) appears exactly 57 times in Sura Qaf. 57 x 2 = 114 - the total number of chapters in the Quran.

checksum.title: The Digital Checksum Analogy ⓘ
checksum.description: Some researchers have proposed that, just as modern digital systems use checksums to protect data integrity, these letters may have served a similar built-in verification function for the Quranic text. This remains an academic hypothesis — the Quran itself does not explain the purpose of these letters.

impossibleRhythm.title: Neither Poetry Nor Prose
impossibleRhythm.intro: In 7th-century Arabia, language existed in two forms: rigid poetry bound to 16 strict meters, or free-form prose. No other category was conceivable. When the Quran arrived, the Arabs were stunned. This text fit none of the 16 meters - yet it was not prose either. It was rhythmic but unmeasured. Disciplined yet free. A form never heard before. Linguists call it sui generis - a category unto itself. The Quran's language created its own genre in the history of literature.
impossibleRhythm.poetryDesc: Rigid structure bound to 16 strict meters
impossibleRhythm.quranDesc: Rhythmic yet meterless - a form never seen before

ijaz.label: The Literary Challenge — I'jaz
ijaz.text: The Arabs called it poetry — the poets rejected it. They called it prose — the scholars refused it. The challenge to "bring something like it" has gone unanswered for 1,400 years.

fasila.title: Fasila: Each Sura's Sound Signature
fasila.desc: Every sura in the Quran has its own distinctive closing sound — called 'fasila'. This pattern at the end of each verse is neither bound by rhyme rules nor accidental. It is a sound architecture.

duha.title: Sura Ad-Duha — The '-â' Fasila
duha.note: The first 8 verses end with the same long '-â' sound. No rhyme scheme is imposed; yet the sound pattern flows as a natural extension of the meaning.

examples.detail: The dominant closing sound remains consistent across 62 verses — a sonic coherence no Arabic poem of this length has achieved.
After every two verses, the refrain 'Which of your Lord's favors will you deny?' returns — fasila and refrain working together, interwoven.
examples.infoText: Refrain: A line or stanza repeated at intervals in poetry or music. In Ar-Rahman, this verse is repeated 31 times.

examples.detail: The '-în / -ûn' sounds dominate as the closing pattern across all 55 verses. Each section closes with the refrain 'Is there any who will take heed?'
examples.infoText: Refrain: A line repeated at intervals in poetry. In Al-Qamar, 'And We have certainly made the Quran easy to remember — so is there any who will take heed?' (54:17) is repeated 4 times.

impossibleRhythm.quote: A rhythm without meter, freedom within discipline.

soundArchitecture.title: These Sounds Are No Coincidence
soundArchitecture.intro: Read the verses about punishment aloud. You will hear harsh, explosive consonants: ق ك ط ص. Now read the verses of mercy and paradise. You will hear soft, flowing sounds: م ن ل ر. This contrast is not accidental — linguistics calls this phenomenon 'phonetic symbolism.'

phonetics.title: Phonetic Symbolism
phonetics.description: Linguists and phoneticians have observed that plosive consonants (ق ك ط) create physiological tension, while nasal and fricative sounds (م ن ل ر) facilitate relaxation. This phenomenon, known as phonetic symbolism, is found across many languages. In the Quran, the pattern shows a systematic correspondence between a verse's subject and the texture of its sounds.

soundArchitecture.closing: The Quran communicates through sound BEFORE meaning.

hiddenSymmetry.title: A Mirror Within a Mirror
hiddenSymmetry.intro: Consider Sura Al-Fatiha. Seven verses. Seems simple. But look at its structure: a perfect mirror symmetry. A-B-C-CENTER-C'-B'-A'. Ring composition is a structure that could only be systematically identified through modern literary analysis — the kind developed by scholars like Mary Douglas in the 20th century. For 1,400 years, commentators wrote volumes without naming this framework — the structure was there, but it had no name. How a text revealed in fragments over 23 years carries such consistent architecture remains an open question.

layers.label: A — Allah's Name
layers.text: Opens with the name of Allah

layers.label: C — The Most Merciful
layers.text: The Most Merciful, the Most Compassionate

layers.label: C' — Worship
layers.text: You alone we worship (response to Mercy)

layers.label: A' — Those Who Received Grace
layers.text: Those who received grace (response to Allah's name)

stat.label: Academic Reference
stat.author: Raymond Farrin
stat.description: Raymond Farrin's Structure and Quranic Interpretation (2014) is among the most comprehensive academic analyses to identify widespread ring composition structures in the Quran. These findings raise questions about the text's internal architecture.

hiddenSymmetry.closing: This structure could only be formally named in the modern era. But it had always been there — unnamed, within the text, from the very beginning.

sevenLayers.title: One Verse, Seven Universes
sevenLayers.intro: Verse 35 of Sura An-Nur is one of the most extensively interpreted verses in the Quran. Ibn Arabi, Ghazali, Razi - the great scholars wrote volumes on this single verse. Why? Because like light passing through a prism, this verse reveals a different reality from every angle.

verse.translation: Allah is the Light of the heavens and the earth...

layers.title: Literal (Physical)
layers.description: Light, lamp, glass, olive oil - a physical description of illumination

layers.title: Metaphoric (Spiritual)
layers.description: Divine guidance as light piercing through darkness

layers.title: Cosmological (Scientific)
layers.description: Light as the fundamental force of the universe, photons, the electromagnetic spectrum

layers.title: Epistemological (Philosophical)
layers.description: Knowledge as illumination, ignorance as darkness

layers.title: Psychological (Inner World)
layers.description: The inner light of the soul, consciousness, awareness

layers.title: Mystical (Sufi)
layers.description: The Sufi concept of divine light (Nur al-Muhammadi), stages of enlightenment

layers.title: Theological (Divine)
layers.description: Allah as the source of all existence, light beyond light

sevenLayers.closing: These seven layers demonstrate how deeply multi-dimensional a single verse can be. Each perspective illuminates a different reality.

scientificSigns.title: What Science Discovered 1,400 Years Later
scientificSigns.intro: The Quran is not a book of science. But certain verses point to realities that modern science would not discover for centuries. Each example is accompanied by an honest question.

iron.title: The Cosmic Journey of Iron
iron.content: The Quran chooses an extraordinary verb for iron: anzalnā — We sent down. Not created, not formed: sent down. In the 7th century, this would have seemed illogical; iron comes from the ground, found in mines. Modern astrophysics explains why this choice is correct. Iron cannot form in medium-sized stars like our Sun — the required fusion energy is simply not available. Iron is only synthesized at the end of massive stars' lives, in supernova explosions, then scattered into space. The iron on Earth arrived via meteorites from such an explosion billions of years ago. The correspondence between this word choice and astrophysical reality is something scholars have interpreted in various ways across centuries.

verse.arabic: وَأَنزَلْنَا الْحَدِيدَ فِيهِ بَأْسٌ شَدِيدٌ وَمَنَافِعُ لِلنَّاسِ
verse.translation: We sent down iron, wherein is great might and benefits for humanity.

facts: Hadid 57:25 — the verb 'anzalnā' (We sent down) is used specifically for iron
Iron cannot form in stars like our Sun: insufficient temperature and pressure for fusion
Iron is only produced in supernovae and scattered into space
All iron on Earth arrived via meteorites billions of years ago

Sura Al-Hadid is the 57th chapter; Fe-57 is one of iron's stable isotopes — a noted coincidence. However, iron's most abundant isotope is Fe-56 (~91.75%); this numerical overlap cannot be presented as scientific verification
iron.criticalNote: Whether 'anzalnā' in this context signifies cosmic origin is disputed. The majority of classical commentators interpreted this verb to mean 'We bestowed' or 'We provided.' The astrophysical interpretation is a reading that became prominent in the 20th century.

universe.title: The Expanding Universe
universe.content: In 1929, Edwin Hubble measured that galaxies are moving away from us. The universe was not static — it was expanding in every direction. This was one of the greatest discoveries in the history of science. Verse 47 of Sura Al-Dhariyat states: 'We constructed the heaven with power, and indeed We are its expander.' The Arabic word mūsi'ūn is grammatically notable: in the active participle form, it denotes present tense and continuity — not a completed past action, but an ongoing, active process. In Sura Al-Anbiya 21:30, the words ratq (joined) and fatq (split apart) point to the heavens and earth separating from a single whole. The correspondence between this linguistic structure and modern cosmological findings is an area of ongoing academic interest.

verse.translation: We constructed the heaven with power, and indeed We are its expander.

facts: 1929: Edwin Hubble observed galaxies receding, proving the universe is expanding
Mūsi'ūn — active participle, present tense, indicating continuous and active expansion
Not a static description — an ongoing process, linguistically aligned with Hubble's dynamics
Al-Anbiya 21:30 — ratq (joined) and fatq (split apart): heavens and earth separating from a single whole
This interpretation became prominent in the 20th century; classical commentaries read it in different contexts
universe.criticalNote: The cosmic expansion interpretation is supported by scholars including Mawdudi and Sayyid Qutb, as well as some contemporary academics. However, some linguists argue that 'mūsi'ūn' in classical usage means 'one who possesses power and capacity.' Both interpretations are linguistically defensible.

ocean.title: The Ocean Barrier
ocean.content: Two distinct bodies of water flow side by side, yet they do not mix. An invisible boundary separates them. This is what modern oceanography calls a halocline: a natural boundary layer formed by differences in salinity, density, and temperature. The Atlantic and the Mediterranean at the Strait of Gibraltar, and the Black Sea and the Sea of Marmara at the Bosphorus, separate in exactly this way. The Quran describes this phenomenon in two separate verses: in Al-Rahman 55:19-20, two seas meet without transgressing each other; in Al-Furqan 25:53, a barzakh (barrier) lies between them. When compared to modern oceanographic terminology, the technical precision of these descriptions is striking.

verse.arabic: مَرَجَ الْبَحْرَيْنِ يَلْتَقِيَانِ ﴿١٩﴾ بَيْنَهُمَا بَرْزَخٌ لَّا يَبْغِيَانِ
verse.translation: He released the two seas, meeting side by side. Between them is a barrier which they do not transgress.

facts: Halocline: a natural boundary layer caused by differences in salinity and density
Observed scientifically at the Strait of Gibraltar, the Bosphorus, and the Red Sea
Al-Rahman 55:19-20: two seas meet but yabghiyān — they do not transgress upon each other
Al-Furqan 25:53: between them is a barzakh (barrier) and hijr mahjūr (forbidden boundary)
Barzakh: an impassable intermediate zone — corresponds precisely to the halocline definition
ocean.criticalNote: Quranic statements about the seas are exaggerated in some popular narratives. The widely cited story that Jacques Cousteau discovered this verse and converted to Islam cannot be verified in the sources; no such statement appears in Cousteau's own writings. The interpretation presented here is based solely on oceanographic data and the Quranic text.

embryo.title: Embryology — The Word 'Alaqah'
embryo.content: The Quran describes the second stage of the human embryo with the word alaqah. This word simultaneously means three different things in Arabic: a clinging substance, a blood clot, and a leech. Modern embryology confirms that all three descriptions physically apply to the early embryo. In the 7th century, there were no microscopes; the implantation process was invisible to the eye; the embryo measured just 1-2 millimeters. Meanwhile, the era's medical authority Galen taught that muscles form before bones. The Quran in Al-Mu'minun 23:14 states that bones form first, then flesh covers them. Modern embryology vindicates the Quran, not Galen.

verse.arabic: ثُمَّ خَلَقْنَا النُّطْفَةَ عَلَقَةً فَخَلَقْنَا الْعَلَقَةَ مُضْغَةً فَخَلَقْنَا الْمُضْغَةَ عِظَامًا فَكَسَوْنَا الْعِظَامَ لَحْمًا
verse.translation: Then We made the sperm-drop into a clinging clot, and We made the clot into a lump of flesh, and We made the flesh into bones, and We covered the bones with flesh.

meanings.explanation: The embryo clings to the uterine wall — implantation, days 6–12
The early embryo appears as a red mass as blood vessels are forming
The embryo resembles a leech in its C-shape and rounded head; it feeds from the mother

facts: Alaqah — three simultaneous definitions: clinging substance / blood clot / leech; all three apply to the early embryo
Days 6–12: embryo clings to the uterine wall — matches 'clinging substance'
Al-Mu'minun 23:14: bones form first, then flesh — the opposite of Galen; modern embryology confirms the Quran
Dr. Keith L. Moore (The Developing Human, 5th ed.): noted the correspondence between Quranic embryological descriptions and modern science as remarkable
embryo.criticalNote: Keith Moore's interpretations are disputed in the scientific community; some academics argue that Quranic descriptions may reflect the Greek medical traditions of the era. The linguistic interpretation of alaqah, however, is strong on its own terms and can be evaluated independently of Moore.

historicalProof.title: Three Stories Confirmed by History
historicalProof.intro: For centuries, details in the Quran were dismissed as 'errors.' Modern archaeology and linguistics proved them right. Here are three striking examples.

pharaoh.title: The Pharaoh's Body
pharaoh.subtitle: ~1200 BCE — Confirmed in 1881
pharaoh.content: Sura Yunus 10:92 - God tells the drowning Pharaoh: 'Today We will preserve your body so that you may be a sign for those who come after you.' A strange promise. The Torah makes no mention of the Pharaoh's body being preserved. It simply says he 'drowned' and closes the story.
points: The Torah is silent about the Pharaoh's body (Exodus 14:28 - just 'drowned')
1881: 3,000-year-old mummies discovered at Deir el-Bahari
Ramesses II's mummy is now displayed at the Cairo Egyptian Museum
Salt crystals found in the body (possible seawater evidence)
In the 7th century, the existence of these mummies was unknown

verse.arabic: فَالْيَوْمَ نُنَجِّيكَ بِبَدَنِكَ لِتَكُونَ لِمَنْ خَلْفَكَ آيَةً
verse.translation: Today We will preserve your body so that you may be a sign for those who come after you.

haman.title: The Haman Code
haman.subtitle: ~1300 BCE — Confirmed in 1935
haman.content: For centuries, critics claimed the Quran contained an 'error': the name Haman. In the Torah, Haman lives in the Persian Empire (~480 BCE). In the Quran, Haman appears alongside the Pharaoh (~1300 BCE). An 800-year discrepancy!
points: 800-year discrepancy between the Torah's Haman and the Quran's Haman
Rosetta Stone (1799) → Champollion (1822) → Hieroglyphs decoded
Ranke's encyclopedia (1935): 'Hm-n-h' found in Egyptian records
Role: overseer of stone workers, construction manager - New Kingdom period
The Quran describes Haman as a construction official - historically verified

rome.title: Rome's Impossible Victory
rome.subtitle: 614-628 CE — Confirmed by historical records
rome.content: 614 CE: The Sasanian Persians capture Jerusalem. The Byzantine Empire is on the verge of collapse. No one believes Byzantium can recover. At precisely this time, the Quran offers a startling prophecy.
points: 'Bid'i sineen' (a few years) = 3-9 years in Arabic
From 622 to 628 = 6 years - right within the boundaries
'Adna al-ard' means both 'the nearest land' and 'the lowest land'
The Dead Sea area: 430 meters below sea level - the lowest point on Earth
This geographical fact was only discovered in the 19th century

verse.arabic: غُلِبَتِ الرُّومُ ﴿٢﴾ فِي أَدْنَى الْأَرْضِ وَهُم مِّن بَعْدِ غَلَبِهِمْ سَيَغْلِبُونَ ﴿٣﴾ فِي بِضْعِ سِنِينَ
verse.translation: The Romans have been defeated in the nearest land. But after their defeat, they will triumph within a few years.

livingPreservation.title: The Only Unchanged Text on Earth
livingPreservation.intro: The Quran in Mecca = the Quran in Medina = Istanbul = Cairo = Jakarta. Letter for letter, identical. 1.8 billion Muslims, different denominations, different countries, different languages. Yet they all read the SAME text. Variation: zero.

huffaz.label: Huffaz (Worldwide)
huffaz.description: People who have memorized the entire Quran

variation.label: Textual Variation
variation.description: Zero differences across all copies worldwide

written.title: Written Preservation
written.description: Over 40 scribes during the Prophet's lifetime, the first compilation under Abu Bakr, standardization and distribution to 7 cities under Uthman.

oral.title: Living Preservation
oral.description: Over 10 million huffaz (people who have memorized the entire Quran) worldwide. Each hafiz learned from their teacher, who learned from theirs - an unbroken chain reaching back to the Prophet Muhammad (pbuh).

livingPreservation.experiment: A thought experiment: if every written copy of the Quran vanished overnight, huffaz could reconstruct it letter by letter. This is true of no other book in existence.

birmingham.title: Birmingham Manuscript
birmingham.description: 2015: A manuscript at the University of Birmingham was carbon-dated to 568-645 CE - overlapping with the Prophet Muhammad's (pbuh) own lifetime!

zeroRedundancy.title: Every Word Carries a Purpose
zeroRedundancy.intro: At first glance, the Quran appears to repeat itself. The story of Moses is told in over 30 chapters. But each retelling emphasizes a DIFFERENT aspect. This is not redundancy - it is pedagogical design. Like a textbook: teaching the same subject from different angles.
zeroRedundancy.mosesTitle: The Story of Moses - Different Chapters, Different Lessons

mosesExamples.theme: The rebellion of the Israelites and the theme of ingratitude
Dialogue with Pharaoh: methodology of invitation
Prophet Moses' birth, upbringing and flight
Confrontation with the sorcerers: truth versus falsehood

totalWords.label: Total Words
totalWords.description: Total word count in the Quran (approximate)

zeroRedundancy.comparisonNote: Percentages are approximate; figures may vary depending on the corpus analysis method and the texts selected for comparison.
zeroRedundancy.zemahseriQuote: Every word of the Quran is a treasure. Remove one, and the entire edifice collapses.

cards.title: The Prefrontal Cortex
cards.content: The Quran says the liar will be seized by his 'forelock' - the forehead (Al-Alaq 96:15-16). Modern neuroscience: the prefrontal cortex (directly behind the forehead) is the center of lying, moral reasoning, and deception. Brain functions were unknown in the 7th century.

cards.title: Fingerprints
cards.content: 'We are able to restore even his fingertips' (Al-Qiyama 75:4) — God's answer to resurrection-deniers, demonstrating power to recreate in the finest detail. Some contemporary commentators have noted a connection to fingerprint uniqueness; classical exegesis, however, interprets this verse as being about the power of resurrection.

cards.title: Modular Narrative
cards.content: Moses' story is distributed across 30+ chapters in 10 distinct 'blocks.' Each block reads independently, yet together they form a unified whole. Modern cinema calls this 'non-linear narrative.' 1,400 years before Pulp Fiction.

cards.title: The Word Map
cards.content: The root for 'faith' (iman): 811 occurrences. The root for 'disbelief' (kufr): 525 occurrences. Ratio: 1.54 - a positive bias. Mercy: 294. Punishment: 343. The root for 'knowledge': 1.1% of all words. The Quran has a richer word distribution than Shakespeare (hapax legomena: 18-20% vs 15%).

cards.title: Time Flexibility
cards.content: In the Quran, future events are described in past tense (Prophetic Perfect) - as though they have already occurred. And past events are narrated in present tense (Historical Present) - as though happening now. The Sleepers of the Cave: 300 solar years = 309 lunar years. The difference is the exact solar-lunar calendar conversion.

cards.title: Iltifat (Shifts in Perspective)
cards.content: Sura Al-Fatiha uses 3 different perspectives in just 7 verses: third person (He), second person (You), first person plural (We). In modern literature, this is known as 'polyphonic voice.' Achieving this in 7 verses is unprecedented in literary history.

conclusion.title: Seeing the Invisible
points: Its words carry mathematical balance
Its sounds guide emotions
Its structure forms mirror symmetries
Each verse holds seven layers of meaning
It pointed to scientific truths 1,400 years in advance
Its historical details are confirmed by archaeology
Not a single unnecessary word among its 77,800
Preserved letter by letter, unchanged for 1,400 years
conclusion.summary: Its words balanced. Its sounds emotional. Its structure symmetric. Its meaning layered. Unchanged for 1,400 years.
conclusion.question: Could this text be the work of a human being?
conclusion.verseIntro: The Quran asks this very question:

verse.arabic: أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ ۚ وَلَوْ كَانَ مِنْ عِندِ غَيْرِ اللَّهِ لَوَجَدُوا فِيهِ اخْتِلَافًا كَثِيرًا
verse.translation: Will they not reflect upon the Quran? If it had been from anyone other than Allah, they would have found within it much contradiction.

humanDefinition.title: How Does It See You?
humanDefinition.subtitle: Across 6,236 verses, the Quran defines, classifies, and seeks to transform the human being.
humanDefinition.intro: The Quran descends on two fundamental axes: to introduce Allah and to transform the human being. But the destination of that transformation is not vague — it is precisely defined. Who is the Muʾmin? Who is the Muḥsin? What does Muttaqī mean? These terms are not synonyms; each defines a different level of maturity, a different inner state. This section dissects the anatomy of that definition.
humanDefinition.methodologyNote: The data in this section is based on Quranic verses. Occurrence counts and categories are compiled from classical tafsir sources and may vary slightly between scholars. Information drawn from hadith sources is separately marked.
humanDefinition.termsSubtitle: The Quran uses 4 different Arabic words for the human being. Each carries a different layer of meaning — not coincidental, but a deliberate terminological system.
humanDefinition.muminIntro: The Quran's most comprehensive definition of the 'ideal human' appears in Al-Muʾminun, verses 1–11. Seven traits form an interconnected system.
humanDefinition.muminWow1: All traits use the active participle (ism-i fail) — not a momentary act, but a continuous character state.
humanDefinition.muminWow2: "قَدْ أَفْلَحَ" is written in the past tense but refers to the future — emphasis on absolute certainty.
humanDefinition.muminWow3: The sura opens with humility in prayer and closes with preserving prayer — both the beginning and destination: salah.
humanDefinition.oppositionIntro: The Quran frequently defines human types through opposing pairs. This is a structural pedagogical choice: to describe a virtue, it also shows its opposite.
humanDefinition.oppositionNote: In Arabic rhetoric this is called "muqābala" (the art of contrast). The Quran employs it systematically.
humanDefinition.istikaametIntro: The Quran contains hundreds of directives. But the one verse the Prophet described as having aged him is a single verse: Hud 112. Because 'be upright as you have been commanded' is not an action — it is a continuous state.
humanDefinition.istikaametVerse: فَاسْتَقِمْ كَمَا أُمِرْتَ وَمَن تَابَ مَعَكَ وَلَا تَطْغَوْا ۚ إِنَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ
humanDefinition.istikaametTr: "Be upright as you have been commanded — and those who repented with you. Do not transgress. Indeed, He is fully aware of what you do."
humanDefinition.istikaametHadith: The Prophet: "Hud and similar suras have made me old."
humanDefinition.istikaametHadithNote: This account comes from hadith sources (Tirmidhi), not from the Quran.
humanDefinition.istikaametClickHint: Click each word for linguistic analysis
humanDefinition.transformationIntro: The Quran defines a three-stage human transformation. These stages follow one another in sequence, yet are also deeply interwoven.
humanDefinition.transformationHadithNote: The definition of iḥsān ('worship Allah as though you see Him') is from the Hadith of Gabriel — a hadith source (Muslim), not the Quran.

footer.title: Quran Codex
footer.methodology: A synthesis of linguistic, mathematical, and scientific research on the Holy Quran.

sources.name: Raymond Farrin — Structure and Qur'anic Interpretation (2014)
Corpus Coranicum — Berlin-Brandenburg Academy of Sciences
Dr. Keith L. Moore — The Developing Human
Dr. Maurice Bucaille — The Bible, the Quran and Science
Quranic Arabic Corpus — University of Leeds
